metadata.title: Gig / contingent labor - the precarity buffer the jobs headline obscures, and how it distorts the labor statistics
metadata.frame: Gig/1099/contingent work is both a real precarity story AND a measurement distortion. The establishment survey (CES) counts W-2 PAYROLL jobs (plus the birth-death model); the household survey (CPS) counts someone EMPLOYED at >=1 hour/week including gig. So gig work (a) is partly invisible to the payroll headline, (b) lets the unemployment rate (U-3) absorb underemployment, and (c) inflates the 'jobs' count via multiple-jobholders (one person, two payrolls). It is the labor-market analogue of the shelter-CPI lag: the headline misses the lived reality.
metadata.WARNING: EVIDENCE-GRADED. Headline gig counts come substantially from a paystub-vendor aggregator (MakeMyPaystub) and freelance-platform surveys - SECONDARY/promotional, definitions vary (any freelance income vs primary income). Flagged. The structural facts (full-time independents doubling; the Lyft NJ misclassification settlement) are firmer. Kept out of the proofs.
metadata.method: Gig statistics aggregators + legal/enforcement record (state misclassification settlements) + BLS survey methodology (CES vs CPS). Cross-read with macro-official-data-integrity.
cross_refs: macro-official-data-integrity.json (CES vs CPS, birth-death model, the jobs headline)

scale.grade: directional STRONG; exact shares CONTESTED (definition-dependent, vendor sources)
facts: ~70.4M Americans doing freelance/independent work in 2025 (~36% of the workforce per platform surveys); projected ~86.5M (~50%) by 2027.
Workers receiving a short-term W-2 or 1099 ~27% of all jobs in 2024.
FULL-TIME independents more than doubled: ~13.6M (2020) -> ~27.7M (2024) - the firmer, more striking structural number.
Definitions vary widely (any side income vs primary livelihood), so the '36%/50%' headlines overstate full-time gig dependence; the ~27.7M full-time figure is the more conservative anchor.
Up to ~30% of employers misclassify workers as 1099 contractors, saving ~20-40% on labor costs (no payroll tax, benefits, UI, workers' comp).
Estimated ~$3-4B/yr in lost federal tax revenue from misclassification.
Sept 2025: Lyft paid New Jersey ~$19.4M to resolve driver-misclassification/benefits claims (one of many state actions).
Penalty exposure is real (IRS back-tax assessments; CA fines up to ~$25k/violation) - so misclassification is a documented, enforced phenomenon, not a theory.

wage_benefit_loss.summary: #51 - quantify the per-worker 1099/gig penalty vs an equivalent W-2 full-time job. Component figures are fact (BLS ECEC, KFF, Vanguard, SSA); the composite markup is transparent arithmetic.

components.benefit_share_of_comp: BLS ECEC (Mar 2026): benefits = 30.1% of total private-industry compensation ($14.01/hr benefits on $46.61 total; wages 69.9%). So W-2 TOTAL comp ~= 1.43x cash wage - the benefits a cash-only 1099 worker never receives.
components.employer_payroll_tax: FICA 15.3% is split 7.65% employer / 7.65% employee; a 1099 worker pays the FULL 15.3% SECA themselves - an extra ~7.65% employer-side bite out of pocket (partially offset by the SE deduction + 92.35% base). 2026 SS wage base $184,500; Medicare uncapped.
components.employer_health: KFF 2025: avg employer premium contribution ~$7,800/yr single ($9,325 total) and $20,143/yr family ($26,993 total; worker pays $6,850). 1099 workers buy their own.
components.employer_401k: Vanguard How America Saves 2025: avg employer match ~4.6% of pay (median 4.0%); nonmatching contributions avg 5.3%. 1099 workers get $0 employer match.
components.paid_leave: BLS ECEC: paid leave ~$3.44/hr ~= 7.5% of total comp (vacation/holiday/sick/personal). 1099 = zero.
components.ui_workers_comp: 1099 workers are generally excluded from unemployment insurance and workers' compensation (no employer SUTA/FUTA or comp coverage). GAO (dated, illustrative): only ~17% of contingent workers in an employer pension vs ~64% of standard full-time.

wage_benefit_loss.the_markup: Because benefits ~= 30% of total comp, a W-2 worker's total comp ~= 1.43x cash wage; a cash-only 1099 worker must gross ~40-43% MORE just to match total compensation, and absorb the employer 7.65% FICA on top -> roughly ~50% more gross in the full-benefits (family-coverage) case. Equivalently: a gig worker forgoes ~30% of equivalent total compensation PLUS self-funds the ~7.65% employer payroll tax.
wage_benefit_loss.net_earnings_reality: After expenses, gig hourly often lands below W-2 equivalents: UC Berkeley Labor Center (2024) on passenger/delivery drivers; Illinois EPI ~$12.72/hr (Chicago Uber/Lyft after expenses); EPI ~$9.21/hr-equivalent (below minimum wage in 13 of 20 major markets). The contested MIT '$3.37/hr' figure is avoided.
wage_benefit_loss.grade: components FACT (BLS ECEC Mar-2026, KFF 2025, Vanguard 2025, SSA 2026; a few exacts flagged for primary re-pull); the ~30%+7.65% / ~50%-markup framing is transparent arithmetic; net-pay studies vary by metro/method

points: CES (establishment/payroll survey) primarily captures W-2 jobs at established firms + the birth-death MODEL; pure-1099 gig work is largely outside it - so payroll headlines can miss gig labor entirely.

CPS (household survey) counts a person EMPLOYED if they worked >=1 hour for pay in the reference week - so a gig worker scraping a few hours counts as 'employed', holding U-3 down even amid underemployment (U-6 captures more but is less cited).

MULTIPLE JOBHOLDERS: one person with two payroll jobs counts as two 'jobs' in CES - so the jobs-added headline can rise while the number of employed PEOPLE (CPS) stagnates (the CES-CPS divergence seen in 2024-25 alongside the -911k benchmark, macro-official-data-integrity).

Net effect: gig/contingent work lets the labor headline look stronger and tighter than the lived experience of precarious, benefit-less, multi-job income - the same headline-vs-reality gap as shelter CPI.

hidden_underemployment.summary: #51 - the 'hidden U-6 equivalent': the published broad measure plus the gig-quality layer it cannot see. Headline counts are fact (BLS May-2026); the quality-adjusted bound is an illustrative, double-count-flagged range (not false precision).
hidden_underemployment.the_gig_layer_u6_misses: U-6 already counts involuntary part-timers and the marginally attached, but it counts a RELUCTANT INDEPENDENT (someone whose primary income is gig/1099 who would prefer a W-2 job) as FULLY EMPLOYED. McKinsey: ~62% of independents say they'd prefer permanent employment (2016 Independent Work); 'Reluctants' = 14% of independent workers (2022 American Opportunity Survey), with ~36% of the employed (~58M) doing some independent work. So on the order of ~8M reluctant primary-income independents sit inside 'employed' that a quality measure would treat as underemployed.
hidden_underemployment.honest_bound: Layering the reluctant-independent share onto U-6 implies a labor-QUALITY-adjusted underemployment materially above the published 8.1% - plausibly into low-double-digits - BUT the components overlap (some reluctant independents are already in U-6's involuntary-part-time or secondary-income, not slack), so a single precise number would be false precision. The defensible claim: U-6 (8.1%) is a FLOOR on broad slack; counting benefit-less involuntary gig as underemployment pushes it several points higher, but the exact figure is definition-dependent.
hidden_underemployment.grade: headline counts FACT (BLS May-2026; multiple-jobholder headcount flagged for FRED re-pull); McKinsey shares are 2016/2022 vintages (labeled); the quality-adjusted bound is illustrative, not precise

bubble_link: The AI build-out (the proven core) is simultaneously a labor story: AI targets exactly the knowledge/creative/customer-service tasks that gig platforms intermediate, while gig work is the buffer absorbing displaced or underemployed workers without benefits or job security. A 'strong jobs' headline built partly on multiple-jobholders and gig hours can coexist with deteriorating labor quality - which the bubble's productivity narrative needs to obscure.

edges.notes: ~30% of employers misclassify (save 20-40% labor cost); ~$3-4B/yr lost tax; Lyft NJ ~$19.4M settlement (Sep 2025).
Gig invisible to CES payroll; CPS counts >=1hr/wk as employed; multiple-jobholders inflate the jobs count -> headline stronger than lived reality (ties the CES-CPS divergence + -911k benchmark).

assessment: Gig/contingent labor is both real precarity and a statistical fog machine. The firmest figure is full-time independents roughly DOUBLING (13.6M->27.7M, 2020->2024); the splashier '36%/50% of the workforce' counts are vendor-survey, definition-dependent, and flagged. Misclassification is documented and enforced (Lyft's $19.4M NJ settlement; ~$3-4B/yr lost tax; ~30% of employers). The measurement consequence is concrete: pure-1099 work sits largely outside the payroll (CES) headline; the household (CPS) survey counts >=1hr/week as employed, so U-3 absorbs underemployment; and multiple-jobholders inflate the jobs-added count - exactly the CES-CPS divergence that accompanied the -911k 2025 benchmark (macro-official-data-integrity). It is the labor twin of the shelter-CPI lag: the headline looks healthier than the lived reality. And it is the buffer absorbing the very workers the AI build-out threatens. Overlay; not used in the proofs.
